segments.text: Yeah.
Rob. No, it's not a name. It wasn't the budget.
I think
they're called robins. What's the name?
We'll call lots of things.
Morning, Minister.
Good morning.
Morning, Ian. Good morning. Glad to have you all with us this morning.
We'll hand over to you for some opening comments, and then we'll get into it.
Well, good morning, Mr. Chair, and good morning, committee members.
Great to be with you this morning. With me at the table today is Ian Rennie, the
Secretary of the Treasury, and I believe Chris Bunny, the Deputy Secretary, is
just behind me, as well as relevant officials from the Treasury,
who are here to help with your questions if need be.
As the Chair has permitted, I'll make some brief introductory remarks.
On budget day, people focus on new spending, which is perfectly
understandable. It is important to remember, however, that this is only a
portion of total government spending, just as an iceberg has a
visible portion above water and a large bulk underneath it.
Next year, core Crown expenses in total, that full
iceberg, will total around 155 billion, over
70% of which will go to health, education, welfare, and
superannuation. Another 6% will go to servicing
the government's debt.
And that all adds up to a big number.
Core Crown revenue, mostly from taxpayers, will be around 145
billion. So there is a $10 billion difference between those two
numbers. That is the deficit, and we will have to borrow to
meet it. In comparison, the amounts we focus on in the budget,
like net new discretionary spending of $2.1 billion,
are relatively small. But that is the point.
The government's approach is to keep new budget spending tight,
forcing a hard look at the underlying 150-odd
billion of spending, 150-odd billion, to see where this money could
be better spent. And better spent for this government
almost always means spending it on our priority areas
of health, education, law and order, defense, and
infrastructure.
Keeping a tight rein on discretionary new spending and
reprioritizing across government means we are continuing to
invest in frontline public services, providing temporary,
timely, and targeted funding to respond to the fuel crisis, and
maintaining a pipeline of infrastructure investments.
And at the same time, we are repairing the government's books.
That repair is necessary because there has been a long period of
operating deficits and growing debt, and that situation will
not resolve itself automatically as the economy recovers.
Specifically, the government is aiming through its fiscal strategy to
reduce core Crown expenses towards 30% of GDP, to
return the headline operating balance measure, OBERGAL X, to surplus by the
'28, '29 fiscal year, and to put net core
Crown debt as a percentage of GDP on a downward
trajectory towards 40%.
Members are aware that Treasury's recommended net debt ceiling is
50% of GDP, and that seems to have bipartisan
support, or at least it used to. We are aiming for
40% because the ceiling should never be the target.
History shows that one economic shock on its own can push up debt
by 20% GDP.
That is precisely the point of having moderate amounts of debt, so
it can increase significantly in a crisis or a shock, but
not cripple the country with unmeetable debt servicing
costs. But once that buffer is used, it
does have to be restored again. So how are we tracking
against our fiscal goals? The Treasury's budget forecasts
show core Crown expenses falling to 30.3% of
GDP by the end of the forecast period, well on the way to
the 30% target.
OBERGAL X is forecast to return to surplus in the '28,
'29 target year, and that is a year earlier than previously
expected in the half-year update.
And net core Crown debt is forecast to peak at
46.1% of GDP in the '27, '28 fiscal year,
again, lower than at the half-year update, then decline to
44.4% of GDP by the end of the forecast period.
In other words, the debt curve finally stops going up and
starts going down. We are making progress on the debt
mountain.
This improvement in the books is reflected in the government's borrowing program.
New Zealand Debt Management has lowered its forecast issuance of government
bonds by $6 billion over the next four years.
This is, members, the first downward revision to the bond program since
There've been a lot of headwinds to the government's fiscal strategy over the last
two and a half years from external economic events over which we
have no control. Despite this, the fiscal
strategy remains on track, and that's because the government
has taken care with public money and made what are tough choices.
Since this is election year, the next round of forecasts will be in the PREFU,
the Pre-Election Economic and Fiscal Update.
I can announce today that the PREFU will be on Tuesday the 29th
of September. That is in the week after the House rises, and
is five and a half weeks before the election. I'm happy to take your questions.
Thank you, Minister. We've got Barbara Ann first.
Good morning, Minister. I do acknowledge this is your third budget, and to Ian
Rennie and the team,
I totally acknowledge that difficult budget to put together given the
uncertainties.
Minister, just having a look at between Budget '24 and '26,
how many more people are on the job seeker benefit or emergency
benefit between those two forecast periods?
So the place to look is if you go to
the, um-
The budget
economic fiscal update, I think, has the table.
Certainly, I have the table available here.
So,
yeah. Thank you, Mr. Rennie. So, if we look at
the numbers, they are on page 116 of the BFOU.
And if we look at the actual numbers for the reference year that you gave was
2024, their numbers on JobSeeker support and
emergency benefit were 194,000. As
we are here today, the actuals in 2025 were
217, and the forecast for '26 is
Yeah. So 98,000 extra. So what's the fiscal cost
of 98,000 people on the job seeker's benefit
across the period to 2028?
Well, again, if you look at the table above that, the welfare benefit expenses,
in the BFOU, that sets that out. And I think it
is clear to everyone that reducing unemployment has not
only a fiscal cost but a significant social cost, and it
is the objective of this government that we have as many New
Zealanders in work as possible. Which is why we are very
pleased that in these forecasts, we are forecasting
220,000 jobs being added to the economy over the forecast period.
We're forecasting that while unemployment will peak,
partly worsened by the fuel crisis, will peak this year.
It is forecast to continue to reduce throughout the forecast period.
And that is positive for every New Zealander who will have a job, their families
who will be supported through that job and independent income, and it is positive
for the government books.
So perhaps, maybe then for Treasury, what is the fiscal cost?
Well, it's very clearly set out-
Does that include-
... Ms. Munce. You can see that in the table welfare
benefit expenses, which has the costs of the various benefits, including
New Zealand Superannuation, JobSeeker, and emergency benefit.
And those costs are set out in the table above.
So,
the numbers are available for you.
Does that include the accommodation supplement allowance,
winter energy payments, additional Working for Families?
Yes. That table sets out very specifically the amount expected to go
into accommodation assistance, the amount expected to go into Working for Families
tax credits and other tax credits, noting this government
has made some changes to some of those entitlements.
Specifically,
we have increased the maximum accommodation allowance that can be
paid to families receiving it. Also, of course, we
made a significant decision in our first budget to unwind the
indexing of benefits to wages, which had been a decision by the
previous government. We chose instead to index them to
inflation, which has reduced the cost of those
benefits.
So as part of your budget, I think it was last year for
Investment Boost, you said that it was going to-
Sorry, is this a new primary?
It's part of the jobs question.
Okay.
You said that it was going to increase, I think, 240,000 jobs, and this year it's
220,000. Is that correct?
In the BFOU last year, we predicted that over
that forecast period, which is, of course, a different forecast period from
that in this year's BFOU necessarily.
We forecast that over that forecast period, 240,000 jobs would be
created. In this BFOU, as we look ahead over a different forecast
period, we're looking at 220,000 jobs being created in the New Zealand
economy. And I think that that is positive.
It shows that there will be new opportunities for New Zealanders, both those
currently in work
looking to get a new job opportunity, and those who are
currently out of the workforce. And you do see that the unemployment number
correlates, that it reduces over that forecast period.
So what is the increase in the working-age
population over that same period?
I don't have those numbers right here, but what
we do have is a
breakdown of those numbers I'm sure that we can make available to you.
You would expect that there would be an increase in the working-age population over
that period.
And sorry, I'm still going. That's all right.
How many of those jobs are because of the decisions made in Budget 2026?
So directly related to budget decisions as opposed to private
market-
Well, these things are very-
... population moves
... as the member appreciates, these things are very difficult to
disaggregate because
the effect of overall employment levels is a
combination of what's happening with the underlying population, what's
happening with underlying economic growth,
and at the margin, there is also the effect of welfare policies.
It is the case that where governments don't provide adequate
incentives for people to move from welfare into work, then you could expect
structurally higher levels of unemployment.
It is also the case that policies such as Investment Boost, which
in the Treasury and IRD's estimation will increase overall potential
growth, that with higher overall growth comes more job
creation. It's also the case that the investment in infrastructure projects
and the view of the Infrastructure Commission for every billion dollars of public
investment per year in infrastructure, around
4,500 jobs are created.
And it is the case fundamentally that where an economy is growing and
businesses have the confidence to invest either in hiring new
staff, expanding into a new market internationally,
expanding their business here at home, that with that growth
comes new job opportunities. Which is why, as a government, we have
focused on a going for growth agenda because we know that the best thing
that we can do to support more job creation is to overall
grow the economy faster.
So then why have employment forecasts been revised down since the last budget
by 82,000 fewer people forecast to be employed in '25,
There've been a few events in the Middle East which I'd draw the member's attention
to.
In particular,
we have seen a global oil shock that, in the view of the International Energy
Agency, is the most extreme the world has ever experienced.
That has led to significant effects for the New Zealand economy.
First of all, a downgrading in global growth, including from our trading
partners. It has also led to a supply shock in prices,
which for New Zealanders they're already experiencing at the pump, and is
viewed to increase inflationary pressure, therefore meaning
a more contractionary monetary policy stance than may otherwise be the case.
New Zealand is not immune to international events, and there have been significant
international events since the last budget update.
So the budget also forecast shows that real
wage being negative. Why is that?
Well, because of the international fuel crisis,
which is expected in the immediate future to lift
inflationary pressure significantly.
What those forecasts also show is that over the forecast period,
real wage growth is expected to accelerate and overall be
positive.
Sorry, last one
... year '27, 72,000 year '28. Treasury
says in their BEFU that it's short-term, the impact from
that conflict. How can you reconcile that from your last statement?
Sorry, how can I reconcile what?
From your last statement. So you've just said in your last statement because of the
conflict-
Yes
... year '27 it's less than 77,000.
'28 it's less than 72,000. It's fewer by 72,000.
How does that reconcile with-
Are you talking about job numbers?
Yes.
Because there are always, in an economy, lag effects
between an economic shock and the impact on different
indicators. It is an orthodox economic
view that when you have a price shock, and therefore a
growth shock, that the last indicator to recover
typically is unemployment. And so Treasury have applied
a very orthodox forecasting model in which they expect
that as inflation is brought under control and growth recovers,
you will then see employment levels recover and unemployment
levels recover.
Thank you. We've got a new primary from Dan Goodwill.
Thanks, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Minister, and to your team at the Treasury for coming
before us today.
Welcome.
I want to turn the attention to New Zealand's debt position.
Mm-hmm.
As we know from table one, debt's currently
191 billion. As a proportion of the economy, it-
I've got a voice
Mm-hmm.
How worried are you about New Zealand's debt position, and how worried
do you think New Zealanders should be about our debt position?
Well, our debt position is materially different from what it has
been historically. It tripled in nominal terms
following COVID.
Yep.
Doubled as a proportion of the economy.
And for reference, if you look back, Bill English, Michael Cullen, you can pick a
blue or a red finance minister. The orthodox position was to keep debt under
20% of GDP, and it is now far above that.
The thing that's very material, I think, for members to understand is the debt
servicing cost that comes with that on an annual basis and the opportunity
cost that represents. So I'd draw members' attention to page
115 of the BEFU, which outlines core Crown expenses and
shows that this year, debt servicing costs are
forecast to be $9 billion. Now,
obviously, that far overshadows any
discretionary additional spending decisions the government has made in this budget.
It's more than four times our overall operating allowance.
To put that number into context, it is enough to fund a year's
operations of the defense force, the police force, the customs
service, the corrections service, the justice system.
So it is a significant amount of money, and every dollar spent on servicing
debt is a dollar not available to be invested in our health system, our education
system, and public infrastructure.
And so it has been the view of successive governments, and certainly the
view of this government, that ensuring that we get debt down to manageable levels
is important,
not just for our resilience in the world, but also for ensuring that we have
manageable debt servicing costs.
Good. Next question is what feedback you're getting
from ratings agencies,
bond holders, bond issuers, or bond
purchasers around the
current government's approach on
fiscal strategy.
Well, both Moody's Ratings and Fitch Ratings
have looked at New Zealand's debt and deficit
positions and have said that it is essential that we keep to the
fiscal strategy, and track a credible path back to
surplus and debt reduction. They've put us on a negative outlook, which is to
say they've retained our current credit rating, which
is a good one because it has material effects for the cost of
borrowing internationally. But they've said that it is incredibly
important that we show progress on our fiscal
strategy of reducing deficits and debt.
If we don't do that,
what is likely to occur is that those credit rating agencies
would downgrade our credit rating.
And of course, many New Zealanders will have experienced what a downgraded
credit rating means.
What it essentially means is it's harder to borrow and more expensive to
borrow. And so for New Zealand, that would be very material.
It would mean more of the taxpayer dollars that we collect would have to go into
servicing debt rather than investing in the future that we're all trying to
create. So maintaining a prudent position
in the eyes of the credit rating agencies is important actually
for how much it costs to borrow, for the interest rates we pay, not only as the
government, but also that New Zealand businesses and private
borrowers pay. So we do take their
estimations of our books very seriously.
And final question is you've had to make some tough decisions in this budget, but
also the previous budgets.
Mm-hmm.
Can you give us a sense of if you hadn't have Taking those
decisions, what would be the potential impact on
debt interest and potential outlook from rating
agencies?
Yeah. Look, colleagues will be aware that the approach we have
taken across three budgets is to supplement
our operating allowances with a significant program of
reprioritization and saving. And that has been
necessary to keep up with the growing costs that are very real
in our health system, our education system, the very real needs to
invest in infrastructure for the future.
We continue to have an infrastructure deficit in New Zealand, and this government
is determined to repair it. And so, in order to
support those objectives, we have made over three budgets, $50
billion worth of reprioritizations.
And it is simply the case that in the absence of that, you would be
adding $50 billion worth of spending to all of the
numbers in these books. It would mean that the deficits would be far
deeper. It would mean that the debt would be far larger-
Mm
... and the debt servicing costs would be much higher.
I'd wager also that we would be in a position as an economy
where inflation would be higher, and where interest rates would be
higher, too.
And, I would note that decisions in the
past by previous governments not to rein in its spending,
materially contributed to very high inflation rates, which stayed out
of target for 33 months in a row, contributed to one of the
steepest increases in the interest rate path that New Zealanders have
experienced, and very much led materially to the cost of living
challenges that we are still working to unwind today.
Thank you.
Thank you. This seems to be a hot topic.
We've got a few supplementaries, first from Chloe and then Barbara Edmonds.
So Minister, I have a parliamentary written question from you in which I asked what
the revenue impact of the failed ETS auctions would be.
Mm.
And you said, and I quote, "The sale of units through ETS auctions does not have an
impact on revenue. The Crown receives cash proceeds from the sale of ETS
units."
Mm-hmm.
"When auctions are forecast to clear and subsequently fail to, this
results in lower than forecast cash proceeds with a corresponding impact on net
core Crown debt." Can I confirm that your understanding as Minister of Finance is
therefore that the failure of ETS auctions results in the Crown taking on more
debt?
So there's two things happening here.
The first is when ETS units
sell at auction,
yes, that has a cash impact. On the other side, the
sale of a unit also creates a liability for the Crown-
Understood
... which is reflected in the balance sheet.
So, there's a very equal and opposite reaction.
While you get a cash positive impact, you get a liability negative impact
on the other side. The point that you are making about
the cash picture is real, and of course-
Yeah
... it is across all areas of government forecasts that there can be
differences between what is forecast and what eventuates,
and the Treasury always updates that in each of
its updates.
So to that effect, we have looked across the failure of all of the ETS
auctions in the past term of government, and we've compared forecast NZU
cash with receipts with actuals. And what we have come to the conclusion
of is that there's an approximate $1.4 billion increase in debt
that has been taken on as a result of the failure of the ETS auctions.
Does that correspond with your understanding?
Well, I think the simple point that I am working to make to you again,
is that that has also, on the other side, been balanced
out by a commensurate reduction in Crown
liability. So you cannot look at one without
the other. If those auctions had cleared at that price, the Crown's
liability would be higher. So yes, there has been a reduction in cash receipts,
but on the other hand, there has been a reduction in Crown liability.
I also would note
that it's very unclear to me whether the member is saying that the Crown should
intervene in the ETS market to
somehow set a higher price for the auction, which is the
necessary flow on from what she's saying.
Not at all.
And it's our view that actually the operation of the ETS- ...
in a predictable manner is quite important.
And the Crown
has very limited in its ability to affect that price by
intervening in the market.
Just to be clear, Minister, about said member's position.
Mm.
It is that we should have a credible ETS market.
Something which many market players, experts have provided commentary on over the
past two years stating that government policy has resulted in undermining of
confidence in the emissions trading scheme, resulting in the failures of those
ETS auctions. That is my position. That is the position of said market.
The other point that I would make is that I very much understand the correspondence
between liabilities and those cash proceeds.
Mm-hmm.
But the point that you were making in your previous answers was that
increased debt also comes with increased debt servicing.
So therefore, are you not concerned with the increase in debt that has
resulted as a result of these failure of ETS auctions?
My point is that the liability is reduced on the other side.
I think I've made that clearly.
Okay, we've got another supplementary from Barbara Edmonds.
Yeah, just very simply, how much has net core
Crown debts grown on average during the period
2017 and '23, and what are the forecasts between '23 and
So I would turn the member to my favorite table in the
BEEFA-
Same. It's my favorite, too
... which I have many copies of printed, which is
at the very back, and that is the time series of fiscal and
economic indicators,
which goes through all of those measures, including core
Crown debt,
and shows that core Crown debt,
in percentage terms, was in
2025. The only year for which we
have full numbers for this government is
41.9%. It is forecast in '26 to reach
42.4. And look, I'll save the members the delight
of me reading out the rest of the row, but it is all set out very clearly there.
Yeah. So
my understanding from the table at the back, which is, again, my favorite too,
that as-
... a percentage of GDP, excluding New Zealand Super, it's risen from
33.9% to 44.8% between those two
fiscal periods that I asked about.
Are you-
Sorry, what is risen by that?
From '23 to '28.
So what-
... which measure are you talking about?
Sorry?
Which measure are you talking about?
I'm talking about GDP. It's percent of GDP, net core Crown debt.
Right.
So net core Crown debt-
Net core Crown debt in 2023 was-
33.9, excluding New Zealand Super.
Yes.
Well-
So just my question being-
Mm
... given that it's risen from, my understanding, 33.9% to
Mm-hmm
... just understanding the assumptions underlying that for that change.
Yeah. So, in terms of net core Crown debt,
the key issue that we are having to deal with is threefold.
One, as I set out in my opening remarks, the gap
between current spending and revenue.
Now, there have been material impacts on that due to the
delayed recovery that the New Zealand economy has experienced.
Two, significant debt servicing costs, which the government has had
to meet, which go far beyond what the previous government was having to
meet due to the larger amounts of debt that our government inherited.
Three, demographic pressures, which have meant that the cost of New Zealand
superannuation is rising by hundreds of millions of dollars
each year.
Table.
A table that I think is quite helpful is if members have the fiscal
strategy report with them.
We went to some effort to set out on page
22 what has gone into the changes in the
deficit forecast between Pre-FUBT '23 and PFUT
Colleagues will recall it's been well canvassed that there was a
significant downward revision following the overly optimistic
forecast provided in Pre-FUBT 2023.
But what that table also sets out is that many of
the changes that occurred in the deficit position were essentially to do
with underlying economic conditions being different than had been forecast.
Even if you exclude policy decisions to
reduce taxation on working people, tax revenue reduced
significantly over that period. But what you can also
see is that deliberate government decisions to reduce budget
packages and allowances has had a material
improvement to the size of the government deficit.
Which is to say, in the absence of the deliberate decisions by this government, the
deficits would've been far larger, the debt position would be far larger.
Okay, we've got still on supplementaries, Dr. David Wilson.
Yes. Thank you, Minister, for that explanation.
You said it a little bit earlier that we can't outgrow this, and we're just talking
about net core Crown debt-
Mm
... currently at around 41.9 and bouncing around the mid-40s right through to
Mm.
So can you put those two things together for us?
And also in terms of that outlook, we're looking at nominal
GDP already starting to rise and real GDP as well.
Mm.
So that kind of indicates growth. Can you sort of tell us what you mean when you
say we can't outgrow this?
Well, first of all, just let me be very clear that we
absolutely want growth, and we are forecasting growth in
these budget forecasts. Growth will accelerate throughout the forecast period,
reaching 2.3% in the next fiscal year and 3.2% beyond
that. That's very positive. That growth brings jobs, higher
incomes, a real improvement in people's material circumstances.
Alongside it, it does help the government books in that along with greater
growth comes greater revenue for the government.
Also, reductions in some of the Crown's expenses.
So we essentially need to support fewer people on benefits when there's more growth
and more employment. At the same time, however, with
growth some expenses continue to grow.
So for example, the case of New Zealand superannuation,
that is pegged to the after-tax average wage.
So with growth, after-tax average wages will grow faster than would otherwise
be the case, and therefore New Zealand superannuation broadly
reflects growth across the broader economy.
So those expenses won't drop even if we're growing faster.
In fact, those expenses will grow faster too.
So there are different aspects to it.
And when I say that growth alone won't resolve the
deficit, what I mean is that we will need to continue to
trim back areas of spending in order to bring our
books back into balance. That even if you layer over very high
levels of growth into these forecasts, you would still have a gap, which is why our
government has committed to an ongoing program of reprioritizations,
tight allowances, and very careful management of public money.
So you're saying that superannuation, as a percentage of GDP will rise?
Superannuation costs as a line item will continue to
grow even as the economy grows, and therefore create an
opportunity cost that there are less dollars elsewhere to be invested in health,
education, other areas.
So the answer to that was yes.
As a percentage of GDP, yes, we're expecting superannuation costs to continue to
rise.
Okay. Thank you. We've got a new primary from Chloe Swarbrick.
Are you committed to meeting our entire nationally determined contribution under
the Paris Agreement domestically?
Well, we remain committed to the Paris Agreement.
As the member is well aware, the Zero
Carbon Act sets out emission reduction goals which this
government is on track to meet.
Not true.
In an unfortunate event without- Parliamentary,
um, endorsement. The previous Minister for Climate Change,
James Shaw,
significantly increased New Zealand's
commitment under the Paris Agreement to our national
development contribution, well beyond what sits in our
zero carbon statute. And the cost of that
is significant. Um, and now the, the options
for the government to meet that obligation are both through domestic ema-
emission reduction efforts-
Mm-hmm
... or there is the potential, which some seem enamored by, of sending billions-
Absolutely not
... of dollars offshore
Quite the opposite
Um, and our preference has been to focus on domestic emission
reduction efforts.
If I can, Minister, you'll appreciate I only have so many minutes to ask you these
questions, so I'd really appreciate direct answers to these questions.
My question was, do you intend to meet the entire NDC domestically?
Yes or no.
We, we remain committed to the Paris Agreement.
Which means, as you alluded to in your own answer just then, that we either have
to do that through domestic action or through offshore mitigation.
Do you accept, therefore, that every time the government cuts domestic climate
action, that the offshore mitigation bill increases?
Uh, no, I don't think it's that black and white, and I think that we are one of
several countries who are not on track to meet the NDC.
Uh, that doesn't come into effect until 2030.
We don't currently have a legal liability to send, uh, billions of dollars
offshore, and I suspect that there will be many developments
globally a- about how NDCs are met and managed, uh, over the coming
years.
Are you implying there, Minister, that you expect the Paris Agreement to
effectively fall apart? And if so-
No
... what... Okay, so just, just to be clear then, what
developments... Can you give us any specificity?
What are these supposed developments that might occur?
If not, the basic mathematics, that if we have a nationally determined
contribution, we either have to meet that through domestic action or through
offshore mitigation, and those two things correspond.
If you decrease one, you increase the other.
Well, what I observe is that there are a number of countries
internationally-
Mm-hmm
... who are not on track to meet the NDC requirements-
Mm-hmm
... while also making good progress in reducing their emissions in their home
jurisdictions. Uh, I think that many of those countries will enter
discussions over the next few years about what that means in terms of
the Paris Agreement and how we collectively continue efforts globally, uh,
to reduce emissions. But in a way that also meets the very
real, pressing domestic concerns of a number of
nations. Which is to say, I do not think New Zealand is alone in
saying that it would not meet
the expectations of New Zealand taxpayers if we were to
send billions of dollars offshore, uh, to
meet the terms of a global agreement. Now, we, New Zealand is not alone in that.
Certainly, there are voters around the world who would feel very uncomfortable
about those sorts of choices. And so what I expect is that ongoing diplomatic
efforts will continue.
To be very clear-
Last sup, please.
Yeah, I guess just to be very clear, Minister, given that you've implied my
position many times in your answers there.
The Green Party very strongly-
I, I think I'll be read by my words rather than you, h- the implications you take
from things. 'Cause in my experience, the implications you take from things are
very different from the implications I take from things.
I would like to put my own words in my own mouth, which is-
Sure, you do that, and I'll do the same.
Uh, absolutely.
Great.
So I guess the final point that I would make, or rather the question that I was a-
I would ask, is do you think that it constitutes good economic
management to be making explicit commitments, particularly on the
international stage, that you are not budgeting for nor planning to

critique that. So make up-
I'm sorry.
Make up your mind.
Last supplement.
No, I think you misunderstood, Minister.
I think you may be misunderstanding things.
The point that I am noting is the number of people that are forced into the
position of relying on this. I just wanted a breakdown
so we can understand the suffering that is out there of how many people
who are receiving this
increased expenditure that you're having to account for because of the number of
people that are out of work in this country are also receiving
additional support or food grants.
It's not I'm critiquing the approach, I'm wanting to understand, and with all due
respect, this is your responsibility as Minister of Finance.
It's in the supplementary estimate.
So, there are two things there. The first is, yes, you are
right. The welfare entitlements which we have in
place as a government mean that while people are able to access base
benefits,
they are also able to, in some clearly
regulated circumstances, access additional support,
where they have significant changes in their material position.
It's also the case that the supplementary estimates reflect
the costs of those payments. And I think it is appropriate, Mr.
Chair, that if the member wants to ask more questions about the benefit system
and its operation,
the numbers underlying those figures, then that is a matter for the social
development estimates.
Thank you. We're going to go to a new primary now from Tom Rutherford.
Great. The budget had a capital expenditure package of around
$7 billion of new capital spending, offset by about $1.3 billion
of savings for a net capital impact package of
$5.7 billion. How did you decide on the amount of capital?
Well, look, what we saw in this year's budget
was a real commitment by this government to
continue our investment in the infrastructure pipeline.
There has been discussion across the parliament and across our business and
wider community about the need for New Zealand to have a longer-term
approach to funding infrastructure.
So on becoming government, we set about
determining those long-term plans.
So in education, we've set a multi-year plan for what investment's
required. In health, we've done the same.
Across a number of government agencies, we've required a sense of what does the
pipeline look like.
Then we've set, as I discussed with opposition members earlier,
some clearer requirements around the circumstances in which we'll fund
infrastructure. So clearer requirements for business cases, for
consenting, for deliverability.
When we then looked at the overall capital demands coming into this year's
budget, we wanted to square up those long-term pipelines with what was
deliverable, what had good business cases,
and what was still achievable within our debt ceiling.
And that is what ultimately determined the shape of the overall capital
budget.
Some people say, "Well, you should just invest
whatever you want in capital, that it's fine to borrow for
capital." There are two real constraints on that, and we've experienced them
in recent times. The first is you can get into a situation if you over-commit to
capital, that you end up just inflating the cost of construction.
There's evidence that that occurred under the last government,
but also you get delays in construction because actually there's
not readiness to deliver that infrastructure. So there are limits there.
The other very material limit is around debt.
So, while capital is neutral for the
overall ex position,
it's also the case that it does add to the debt profile.
And so, as you know, we've set that goal of getting the debt curve
bending down, and we have to make sure that our capital allocation decisions are
consistent with that.
Can you just highlight some of the projects included in the capital expenditure
program?
Yeah. Look,
very much making material progress on our health infrastructure
pipeline. So you're seeing a new tower block for Whangarei
Hospital. And I give tribute there to Dr.
Shane Reti, who has argued for that for many, many years.
The next stage of work to redevelop our regional provincial hospitals,
Tauranga,
Palmerston North, Hawke's Bay,
as well as other work to continue infrastructure development for health facilities.
Significant investment for education, both to enable new classrooms,
the redevelopment of multiple schools across the country that are worn out and old
and need redevelopment. Purchasing of land in high-growth areas to allow for new
schools
to be developed.
Then significant investment in transport infrastructure.
In particular, we made a decision to
directly accelerate and fund road and resilience projects
needed in areas that have become highly affected by weather events, where we
just don't think it makes sense to keep rebuilding those roads and billing the
taxpayer for that. Let's actually strengthen them now, make them more resilient to
future weather events. So $400 million invested in those projects across the
country. As well as putting the funding commitment to
allow the very, very much delayed, much talked about
extension to the Waikato Expressway to happen, the Cambridge to
Piarere extension. That's one of the most unsafe stretches of state highway in the
country, as the member from Tauranga will appreciate.
Sorry, Bay of Plenty, but you know the Tauranga region, I mean.
That road, on average, has some sort of disruption or closure every 10
days, and that is ridiculous on a piece of road that is so important
to the country's productivity.
And also has literally meant the loss of life
for New Zealanders and their families.
So we're very proud that we've committed that additional funding to getting that
road going. And then, of course, there's also a chunk of capital there for defense
People often highlight the kit that we're buying from offshore, and yes, that's
part of the capability, but actually a significant part of this year's
investments are also in the facilities that we ask our
servicemen and women to live and train in.
And these are men and women who literally put their lives on the line for the
country, and I think they deserve to live in
housing of a basic standard and to train in facilities that are basic
and meet their needs. So, investment there.
Two other projects were new police stations in Greymouth and-
Yes, sorry, I didn't mention the courthouses and the police stations-
Yeah
... and various other things, yeah.
But with police stations in Greymouth and Wanganui-
Yes
... in particular. Why was it necessary to upgrade those facilities in those
communities?
Yeah. In line with our long-term infrastructure pipeline planning approach, we
asked the New Zealand Police to go away and give us a picture
of which police stations across the country need to be
remediated, need to be replaced, and to have a much more logical
pathway for how that's happening, rather than a really ad hoc approach.
They've done a really good business case on that, and very clearly, the
next two cabs off the rank in highest need were Greymouth and
Wanganui. And therefore, based on that business casing and that
pipeline work, that's where we've provided additional investment.
And frankly, our police deserve it, as do the people who
use those police stations.
Quick sub, Nancy Lou.
A very, very quick one. Minister,
the big numbers, the big amounts for the capital projects are exciting, and it
provides a lot of guarantee and assurance for
the construction community. But what does it
translate to day-to-day New Zealanders in terms of certainty for
jobs-
Mm
... and commitment into growing that career?
Yeah. Look, I think the first lens is always,
what are the infrastructure needs that we have in order to
have both the delivery of good public services and the
enablement of a good, productive economy. And that's number one.
So for me, actually having adequate hospital
facilities is pretty core to the delivery of health services, adequate places for
our kids to learn and those things.
And then the secondary layer on that is actually when we're
building that infrastructure and developing that infrastructure, as you say, that
provides jobs, incomes, contract opportunities for New
Zealand firms and New Zealand households.
And so, I think for your average Kiwi, what they will see
in many, many communities across the country is they will see people in hard
hats and hi-vis with spades in their hands and jobs
building infrastructure for the future.
Infrastructure for the future that will improve public service delivery in the
future, will strengthen our economy for the future, but is providing real
jobs right now. And I had this highlighted to me by
someone the other day who lives in the Hutt, actually, and they were saying, "Yeah,
I really
can feel that happening," because of course they drive past the Melling development
regularly. And that's of course about flood protection, but also about a better
roading network. And when you can see that investment happening in your community,
I think it's heartening. The opening of the shared path here in Wellington,
again, people can see things are getting better on an infrastructure basis.
But there's a long list of needs, so we need to make sure that when we make those
investments, we're really careful about how we prioritize them, how we sequence
them.
Fantastic.
Thank you.
Thank you. We'll go to a new primary now from Dr. Deborah Russell.
Oh, thank you.
Mr. Chair, it's 11:04. So-
It's a shortie.
Sorry?
It's a short one.
Well, we're due to finish at-
I thought we finished at 11:15
Oh, 11:15. Okay.
So last-
It's a long session, isn't it?
Last 10 minutes.
So it's a really interesting one. There's a capital injection to the Natural
Hazards Commission-
Mm
... for business case development-
Mm-hmm
... which I'm just really interested in.
So it's for a-
Thank you for this question. I'm very interested in this too.
Delighted.
So it's a business case for a public infrastructure risk
management scheme for The Crown.
Yeah.
So I'm interested in
the thinking on this, because it's something that needs to concern us all on an
ongoing basis. So, the Natural Hazards Commission, Toka Tū Ake,
at the moment it's really for individuals.
It
covers that insurance gap for individuals and gets insurance going.
So putting this,
I know it's only a business case, but I'm just interested in this thinking around
the
public infrastructure
sitting in there.
And also whether you think that might extend, obviously,
government infrastructure, but what about local government infrastructure and
things like that?
Mm.
So I'm just interested, Minister. I'd love to hear about it.
Yeah, great question. So essentially, the
Crown owns a huge amount of assets
that are insurable assets, whether that's your roads, your railways, your
hospital buildings, your schools.
Right.
And what we have at the moment is an absolute
spaghetti-
Yeah
... when it comes to how we approach insuring and managing those
risks. So in some cases, it will be appropriate for a government agency to
say, "Well, we just put our own money to one side to ensure that we could
replace those assets if there was an extreme weather event, an earthquake or
something." In other
cases, they buy an insurance policy.
The level of that insurance varies markedly from one agency
to another. The tally also is that we have around 100
different insurance policies across government, which I think shows
you that we've got really quite a haphazard approach.
Mm.
And so what we see in other jurisdictions, particularly in Australia
but elsewhere, is that they pool all of
that risk financing together into one place to have more of a
uniform risk appetite for the Crown, to have a more centralized
view of overall assets and risk, and therefore to
take an insurance approach accordingly, either through buying external insurance
products or having their own internal insurance pool.
And so what that business case that we've funded NHC
to do is about, is saying to them, "Okay, go away and have a look at the current
arrangements.
Have a look at how others do it around the world, and come back to us with a
business case as to whether or not it is worthy of
changing our approach, that we would get net benefit to the taxpayer out of doing
that, and give us some views on what that would look like." To the second part of
your question,
in time, could you expand that to also include local government assets?
Yes, you could, but we'll do the first bit first.
Great. I'm really interested. I'll be quick Let's see the business case.
But I guess I won't at this stage. But, I'm really
pleased to see this sort of thinking going on.
I guess there's another set of stuff that's going to sit in around
there around some of the
emergency stuff we do.
Mm-hmm.
So I think the stat is that we spend a
hell of a lot on recovery from emergencies-
Yes
... but not a lot on actually adapting to it in advance.
Is that some work that's going to sit in that space too, or is that going to happen
also?
Yeah, look, absolutely, you're right.
So the approach, again, of different agencies to that
question varies significantly.
Mm.
So
one of the anomalies that gets pointed out to me is that KiwiRail fully insure all
of their rail so that they replace it no matter where it is or how used it is,
whereas NZTA take a more circumspect approach to how
they insure their road. But also, you see
variability in that approach around emergency works versus
taking steps in advance to protect assets
from requiring emergency works. And all things
being equal, if you have a really good asset register, you have a really
good assessment of risk, i.e., how likely are these assets to
be impacted by seismic events, weather events, other events.
Therefore, what is the benefit to risk ratio of
investing now versus paying for an insurance product that
invests later? You should be able to make much more logical and
rational decisions.
Yeah.
But my thesis has been because we have such a disparate approach from one
agency to another and not a clear risk appetite
overall, not even a clear view of the data that should inform these decisions,
that we haven't been as able to prioritize those
investments as I would like us to be.
So having a centralized risk management approach, which the NHC
business case is looking at, would allow us to make much clearer whole
of government decisions about when we invest preemptively versus
when we invest after the fact.
Yeah. It's interesting because obviously it's not going to be one size fits all
because thinking of the case of KiwiRail versus NZTA-
Absolutely. It's not going to be the same-
Yeah, but a break in the-
... but at least you can have a similar risk appetite.
Yeah. A break in the rail network can shut the whole thing down-
Exactly
... but a road network can usually work around it.
Yeah. No, that shouldn't have been taken as a critique of the approach.
It's just pointing out there are different approaches.
Yeah. That's right. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. That's really interesting.
Another quick one from Chloe.
For our country, economy, and democracy, Minister, do you believe that extreme
inequality is a bad thing?
Yes.
So based on your response to my questioning in December 2024,
you said that you believe that you, at your job, is to have a
significant role in reducing inequality.
Mm-hmm.
How exactly have you done that?
The number one thing we've done is improve the performance of our education system.
The number one thing any government can do to liberate a
child from their family circumstances, which they inherit and have no
choice over, is to ensure that when they're at school between the hours of 9:00 and
3:00, that they are getting a decent education.
We cannot control what every parent does in every home.
We cannot control what spending choices parents make.
We cannot control what parenting choices they make.
But what we can do is ensure that no matter what home you were born in, when you
turn up at a school in New Zealand, you get a decent go at
getting literacy skills, numeracy skills that are needed for you to contribute
to our community, for you to get a job, and for you to enter further training.
And frankly, in recent years, we've done far too poor a
job of that. Too many kids, particularly the kids from the poorest homes, have been
left behind by our education system.
And I think the number one thing that our government can do to address that is
ensuring that we are giving every kid an hour a day of reading, writing, and maths,
that they are getting access to the structured literacy and numeracy approaches
that work, that the teachers that are teaching them are getting the professional
development that allows them to take those approaches, and that we are measuring
ourselves against whether kids are learning more.
And so I think Erica Stanford's education reforms, and the success
that they are having, is one of the biggest things we can do for long-term equality
in this country.
I just respectfully, Minister,
we have the most people unemployed since I was born, and they are staying
unemployed for longer. We also have 50,000 more children living in material
hardship than three years ago, and we also have decisions under your
last budget for 129,000 households in social
housing to be approximately $24 per week worse off.
Do you not accept what all of the international and domestic research shows
that per your point on education being the pathway out of poverty, that it's
actually incredibly difficult for children to learn when they are in impoverished
circumstances, which is exactly what your budgetary decisions have created?
I believe children, when given the ability
to be educated well, have the potential to go
well beyond the circumstances into which they are born, and there are examples of
that from all over this country, all over the world.
Kids born to the most difficult circumstances that are
educated well, and actually often also have a mom or a dad or a
caregiver that
says that they are capable of anything and supports them to be capable of anything,
who overcome their circumstances.
And that optimism about the ability of people to shape their own individual
lives lies at the heart of my politics, and I believe that the
state has a very real role in ensuring that kids
can access a good education. If they can't, we are trapping them in the
circumstances to which they are born forever. So yeah, I stand by my view.
Education really matters, and I have no
time for the bigotry of low expectations that persists on
the left that says, "Well, if you're from a low income home, well, it's just too
hard for you."
That's not what I said at all.
My view is that if we educate kids well, so many young
New Zealanders are capable of overcoming the circumstances which they are born
into.
Thanks, Chloe.
Respectfully, my point was you've made those material circumstances harder-
You made your point, and I've made mine.
Thanks, Chloe.
So, Minister, when was the last
time you went to a food bank?
I'm very happy to check my ministerial diary.
When was the last time you went to a state house and sat down with a family and
spoke to them about their issues?
Minister to chair, I think we've now entered the stunt part of the hearing.
Actually, I have one last question, Minister, if I may.
I reserve the right to the last question.
Aside from getting into surplus a year earlier than forecast, what
key things from Budget '26 can New Zealanders take solace in?
Good reading.
Well, I think the fact that having been hit by a number of curveballs,
from tariffs through to a crisis in the Middle East, we are still seeing this
economy bouncing back strongly because the foundations are in order.
That we can expect accelerating growth, inflation coming back in
band, that we can expect 220,000 jobs to be
created, and that all of that can be achieved while we're also seeing big
steps forward in our public infrastructure and public service delivery,
and that we can see all of that happen without the addition of a wealth tax, a
capital gains tax, or higher taxes on working people.
We are making progress as a country, and the worst thing we could do is
put that all at risk by taking a huge diversion to a government that
would wish to torpedo the surplus, rebuild the debt mountain, and
slather you in more taxes.
Thank you for your time, Minister.
Thank you, Minister.
Thank you.
Members, we're going to have a short break until 11:30.
